coredata.dc:description: A tumor microenvironment-responsive wireless strain-pressure hydrogel sensor based on pH-induced controllable nanoparticles was designed for cancer detection in vitro–in vivo model, with excellent ability to distinguish between cancer and normal cells. The pH-responsive nanoparticles (CD-PNB), comprising diol–diol crosslinked semiconducting carbon dots (CDs) and non-conductive polymer (PNB), are sensitive to acidic tumor microenvironments and play crucial role in demonstrating tumor-selective strain-pressure responses. Upon application of strain and pressure, CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel produced distinct electronic signals in the presence of cancer cells (HeLa, PC-3), exhibiting higher strain–pressure sensitivity compared to the normal cells (MDCK, CHO-K1). The strain and pressure gage factors for cancer-cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel were found to be 0.7439 and 5.3052 kPa−1, which were higher than normal-cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (0.5009 and 4.2720 kPa−1). CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel demonstrated excellent response in tumor-bearing mice based on in situ and ex situ measurements, with no inflammation during hydrogel implantation. Moreover, wireless sensing system was used along with CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel to simplify monitoring process and obtain real-time conductivity and strain–pressure profiles on smartphone. Thus, this approach constructs a tumor microenvironment-responsive strain–pressure hydrogel sensor and presents potential for sensitive and selective tumor detection in point-of-care diagnostic applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A tumor microenvironment-responsive wireless strain-pressure hydrogel sensor based on pH-induced controllable nanoparticles was designed for cancer detection in vitro–in vivo model, with excellent ability to distinguish between cancer and normal cells. The pH-responsive nanoparticles (CD-PNB), comprising diol–diol crosslinked semiconducting carbon dots (CDs) and non-conductive polymer (PNB), are sensitive to acidic tumor microenvironments and play crucial role in demonstrating tumor-selective strain-pressure responses. Upon application of strain and pressure, CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel produced distinct electronic signals in the presence of cancer cells (HeLa, PC-3), exhibiting higher strain–pressure sensitivity compared to the normal cells (MDCK, CHO-K1). The strain and pressure gage factors for cancer-cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel were found to be 0.7439 and 5.3052 kPa−1, which were higher than normal-cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (0.5009 and 4.2720 kPa−1). CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel demonstrated excellent response in tumor-bearing mice based on in situ and ex situ measurements, with no inflammation during hydrogel implantation. Moreover, wireless sensing system was used along with CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel to simplify monitoring process and obtain real-time conductivity and strain–pressure profiles on smartphone. Thus, this approach constructs a tumor microenvironment-responsive strain–pressure hydrogel sensor and presents potential for sensitive and selective tumor detection in point-of-care diagnostic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and sensitive cancer diagnostics are critical for selecting suitable treatment and avoiding advanced cancer malignancy [1,2]. Numerous methods, including biopsy, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and positron emission tomography–computed tomography (PET–CT), are used for detecting cancer cells [3–5]. However, these approaches have disadvantages, such as complex procedures, the possibility of unnecessary radiation exposure, and the need for advanced equipment and professional staff. Alternative approaches, such as photoluminescence sensing, have been suggested; however, their poor sensitivity and photostability limit their usefulness and practical use [6–8]. In the practical use, the information from PET–CT or MRI are needed to identify the location for initial assessment, and practically during surgery, a simple method for the preliminary test is still required. Consequently, it is highly desirable to develop a simple and biocompatible point-of-care diagnostic system for the rapid and simple miniaturized detection of cancer cells, for example by placing a sensor patch at the tumor site and obtaining immediate diagnosis during tumor surgery.

Because of their unique properties, including biocompatibility, stretchability, and swelling behavior, hydrogels have emerged as versatile materials, enabling their utilization in a variety of applications, including biosensing, actuators, and tissue engineering [9–12]. Depending on the incorporated nanoparticle or the modified polymeric matrix, hydrogels possess specific properties and exhibit sensitivity to various stimuli, including pH, redox, light, and temperature [13–16]. Recently, hydrogels have been widely used to construct stretchable electroconductive materials that are sensitive to mechanical forces, such as strain and pressure, in order to replicate human skin functions [17–21]. However, utilizing a distinct strain–pressure response, such as responses in neutral and acidic pH, and the sensitivity required to distinguish some physiological conditions remains a challenge. Moreover, there are few reports on electroconductive hydrogels that produce distinct responses based on the conditions in the cellular microenvironment, such as the level of reactive oxygen species (ROS), glutathione (GSH), and extracellular pH (pH < 7.4) of cancer cells [22,23]. Designing an electroconductive hydrogel-based sensor that is capable of producing a specific strain and pressure response to conditions in cancer cell microenvironments could be a new and facile strategy for cancer diagnostics. Among stimuli-responsive nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted immense interest because of their outstanding and attractive properties such as biocompatibility, electroconductivity, and high solubility in water [24–28]. Furthermore, CDs can be easily modified using various moieties to achieve specific characteristics. For example, boronic acid and catechol moieties can be conjugated with CDs to achieve pH responsiveness, owing to the reversible diol–diol crosslinking at different pH values. These pH-responsive CDs have been reported to exhibit different properties in the presence of cancer cells and normal cells [29–31]. Integrating pH-sensitive CDs in electroconductive hydrogel sensors could be an effective strategy for cancer cell detection, which is achieved via distinct strain and pressure responses in the presence of cancer cells and normal cells.

In this study, we developed a tumor-selective electroconductive hydrogel sensor based on pH-responsive nanoparticles (CD-PNB@PVA), which produced distinct electronic responses towards applied strain and pressure in the presence of cancer cells and normal cells. The tumor selectivity of this sensor was because of the acidic tumor microenvironment which triggered the cleavage of the diol–diol crosslinking in the pH-responsive hybrid carbon dots (CD-PNBs). This breakage of the crosslinking between the semiconducting CD nanoparticles and the non-conductive PNB resulted in an increase in conductivity of CD-PNB@PVA. The presence of cancer cells can be detected by observing the strain and pressure responses of the cancer-cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel, which were significantly higher and more sensitive than those produced by the normal-cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. In addition, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel could be utilized along with a wireless sensing system to monitor the sensing performance of the hydrogel implanted in the tumor-bearing mice, and the in situ and ex situ sensing data could be obtained directly via a smartphone.

Hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC, MW: 100,000 g/mol), Pluronic (MW: 12,600 g/mol), 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate (DMA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), dopamine hydrochloride, N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAAm), t‑butyl peroxybenzoate, 4-chlorophenyl boronic acid, toluene, hexane, ethanol, and sodium chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Yongin-si, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (0.05% w/v trypsin-EDTA 1X), Penicillin-streptomycin, Minimum Essential Media (MEM), and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI)−1640 medium were purchased from Gibco BRL (NY, USA). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was obtained from Bioneer Corp. (Daejeon, Korea). SYTO-9, 10X binding buffer, Annexin-V-FITC, and propidium iodide (PI) were procured from Life Technologies (Carlsbad, CA, USA). The MDCK mammalian epithelial cell line, CHO-K1, HeLa and PC-3 cancer cell lines were obtained from Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea). The BABL/c-nude mice (8 weeks, male) was obtained from ORIENT-BIO. Corning® Matrigel® (hESC-Qualified Matrix, LDEV-free) was obtained from Life Science, Korea.

1H NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker AVANCE 400 MHz spectrometer (Waltham, USA). Absorbance spectra were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Optizen 2120UV, Mecasys, South Korea). Nanoparticle size was measured using a dynamic light scattering spectrometer (DLS; Zetasizer Nano, Malvern, Germany). The fluorescence spectra were observed using a luminescence spectrometer (L550B, Perkin Elmer, USA). A confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM; LSM510 Carl Zeiss, Germany) and flow cytometer (FACS; Attune NxT, Thermofisher, Korea) were used to observe cellular nanoparticle uptake. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis was performed with a JSM-6700F, JEOL (Japan). Tensile strength was assessed using a universal testing machine (JP/AG-250kNX, Shimadzu, Japan), and rheological property of hydrogel was determined using Rheometer (Thermofisher, Germany). The electrochemical properties were confirmed using an electrochemical impedance spectrometer (EIS; CS350, CorrTest Instrument, China), sourcemeter (Keithley 2450, Tektronik, USA) and LCR meter (4100, Wayne Kerr Electronics, UK). For wireless monitoring, the wireless system was designed using Arduino Uno micro-controller (ATmega328P Processor, 32-bit R3.0) as the sensing part, a smartphone as a real-time data display, and a Bluetooth module (AppGosu) as the wireless communication bridge between Arduino Uno and the smartphone. To amplify the signal from sample, commercial OP-amp. (17,741, Hitachi) was equipped to produce high voltage gain and wide amplitude output over the small signal. The detail circuit diagram and working principle have been reported in our previous work [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dopamine-conjugated HPC carbon dot (CD)
ce:section.ce:para: HPC (1 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of DDW and placed in a hydrothermal chamber for carbonization process in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. The resulted solution was then freeze-dried to obtain the CD(HPC).

To conjugate the dopamine into the CD(HPC), EDCNHS reaction was performed. In brief, EDC (0.0466 g) and NHS (0.0345 g) were added into the CD(HPC) solution (1 g in 250 mL of PBS 2X) and allowed to react 24 h at 25 °C. The solution was then lyophilized to obtain the CD (MWCO: 3.5 kDa). The conductivity of obtained semiconducting CD was then measured, and found to be 0.0182 mS/cm.

The temperature-sensitive Pluronic-grafted-poly(DMA-co-NIPAAM)-boronic acid (PNB) was synthesized based on our previous work [33,34]. Briefly, Pluronic (10 g) and t‑butyl peroxybenzoate (1.51 mL) were mixed in 250 mL of anhydrous toluene and allowed to react for 2 h at 140 °C. Subsequently, DMA (16.05 mL) and NIPAAm (16.166 g) were added into that Pluronic solution and reacted for 24 h at 140 °C. The excess solvent was then evaporated using rotary evaporator, followed by purification process via precipitation in hexane. The precipitate was further dried in a vacuum oven. The obtained Pluronic-grafted-poly(DMA-co-NIPAAM) (5 g) was then quaternized with 4-chlorophenyl boronic acid (0.3107 g) in 150 mL of ethanol at 70–80 °C for 12 h. The solution was purified using dialysis (MWCO: 3.5 kDa) and freeze dry.

The CD and PNB was further conjugated via diol-diol crosslinking between catechol moieties (Dopamine) and boronic acid. Briefly, CD (100 mg) and PNB (100 mg) were mixed in 50 mL of TBS pH 12 and stirred for 24 h at 25 °C. The solution was then lyophilized to obtain CD-PNB. The resulted CD-PNB (1 g) was then dissolved in NaCl solution (5 M, 100 mL) to immobilized Na+ into the CD-PNB via ionic complex interaction and stirred for 12 h in 25 °C. The solution was then dialyzed (MWCO: 1 kDa) and freeze-dried.

PVA (1 g) was dissolved in PBS pH 7.4 (10 mL) and stirred at 90 °C until well-soluble. The CD-PNB (0.3 g) was then dissolved into the PVA solution and poured into the polystyrene mold. The solution was then allowed for freezing-thawing process for 5 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic properties, strain-pressure response and sensitivity of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel in different pH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was treated with PBS pH 6.8 and pH 7.4 solution for 6 h in various temperatures (25°, 37°, 50°, 60 °C). The resistance of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel treated with each pH and temperature was further checked by using EIS, sourcemeter and LCR meter. To determine the strain and pressure sensitivity, the treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was set in UTM and connected to sourcemeter using copper electrode. The change in resistance was recorded simultaneously when various strains (0–200%) and pressures (0–10 kPa) were applied, and the gage factor was calculated via linear calibration curve between strain or pressure vs the resistance change (ΔR/R0). To observe the distinct strain response, a simple stretching was applied reversibly from 0 to 100% strain after the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was connected to sourcemeter. For pressure response, finger press with different forces (soft, medium, and hard pressing), and weight press (10, 20, 50, 100, 250 g) was applied on the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel which was connected to sourcemeter. All the strain and pressure response were denoted as the change of resistance before and after applying forces (ΔR/R0). For the wireless monitoring, the conductivity and strain-pressure response profiles can be obtained using a wireless sensing device and smartphone. The treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was connected to Bluetooth-based micro-controller via copper electrode. When the Bluetooth option on smartphone was activated, the real-time data of conductivity and strain-pressure responses can be received on smartphone as a resistance graph.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro conductivity and strain-pressure response tests of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing performance of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was assessed by treating the hydrogel with HeLa and PC-3 as representatives for cancer cells, also with MDCK and CHO-K1 as normal cell lines. The cell solution (105 cell/mL) was seeded into the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in humidified 5% CO2. The cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was then dip-washed 3 times using PBS pH 7.4 and the hydrogel was subjected for conductivity measurement and strain-pressure test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo performance by in situ and ex situ methods of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The cancer mouse models were developed using HeLa cells. Briefly, a BABL/c-nude mouse (8 weeks, male) was anesthetized and immobilized on a surgical corkboard. The 10 μL of HeLa and MDAMB cell suspension (1 × 106 cells) with Matrigel (10 mL) were injected into the subcutaneous region of each mouse. After 2 weeks, each mouse underwent subcutaneous incision with a 1 cm long incision, and the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (5 × 5 × 1 mm) was applied to the tumor region up to 3 days. After 3 days, the in situ (direct checking on the implanted site) and ex situ (measured after harvested from mice) measurement of implanted CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was conducted using wireless sensing system. All animal experiments were performed in the Carbon Nano-BioMaterials Laboratory of Wonkwang University and were approved by the Animal Review Committee of Wonkwang University (WKU20–101).

The CD-PNB@PVA hydrogels were subcutaneously implanted into the left and right sides of the dorsal regions (n = 2). After 2 weeks, the dorsal skins were resected and subsequently immersed in PBS solution. The histological examinations after hydrogel implantations were conducted on the paraffin-embedded 5 μm sections. The sections were fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin for 24 h and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H/E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the pH-responsive conductive CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well-known that tumor microenvironment possesses lower pH compared to the other normal physiological conditions (pH 7.4) owing to the production of lactic acid as a result of anaerobic glycolysis in tumor [35–37]. By utilizing this distinct characteristic of tumor, we can selectively target and detect the tumor by developing pH-sensitive biosensor. In this work, a tumor-selective electroconductive hydrogel sensor based on pH-responsive nanoparticles was developed, which functions by generating different responses to different pH conditions in the tumor microenvironments. As the hydrogel-based sensors demonstrate flexibility and stretchability, it is expected that our tumor-selective electroconductive hydrogel sensor is also capable to produce distinct responses toward mechanical forces, such as strain and pressure, in tumor and normal microenvironment condition. To achieve pH-sensitivity, pH-responsive hybrid nanoparticles were synthesized by employing diol–diol crosslinking between catechol groups in semiconducting CD and boronic acid groups in a non-conductive temperature-sensitive PNB. To increase the electroconductivity, Na+ ions were introduced into CD-PNB utilizing HPC and Na+ ionic complex interactions, and they were subsequently incorporated into the PVA matrix via a freezing–thawing process (Scheme S1) [38]. After the molding and freezing–thawing processes, an electroconductive and stretchable CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was obtained (Fig. S1), which is expected to exhibit tumor selectivity under different pH conditions. The CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel sensor was capable of distinguishing between tumor and normal microenvironment owing to the cleavage of the diol–diol bond between the catechol group in the CD and the boronic acid group in PNB, as a result of more acidic condition. This cleavage resulted in different conductivities and electronic responses when strain and pressure were applied to the treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel because of the unconnected semiconducting CD nanoparticles and non-conductive PNB, which reduced the hindrance to the charge transfer process and improved the conductivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. Furthermore, to easily monitor the distinct conductivity and strain–pressure response in both in vitro and in vivo tests, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel sensor was combined with a wireless sensing system to obtain real-time detection results on a personal smartphone ( Fig. 1
a).

The appearance of aromatic peaks in the range of 7.0–7.5 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum of CD-PNB indicated the presence of boronic acid and catechol moieties (Fig. S2a) [39–41]. To confirm the presence of boronate ester bond in CD-PNB, triethylamine (Et3N) was mixed into CD-PNB to bind with boronate ester in CD-PNB and cause a chemical shift of 1H NMR spectra. With the addition of Et3N, the chemical shifting of those boronic acid and catechol moieties in CD-PNB was observed, which was shifted from 7.0 to 7.5 ppm to 6.2–6.7 ppm, confirming the presence of boronate ester bond in CD-PNB (Fig. S2b) [42]. The boronate ester bond and the cleavage of this bond also observed from FT-IR spectra. The appearance of a peak at 1495 cm−1 in CD-PNB was corresponded to the boronic-catechol complex between CD and PNB, which disappeared because of diol-diol cleavage after treated in pH 6.8 (Fig. S3) [43]. Moreover, UV–vis spectrum of CD-PNB revealed the presence of an absorption peak at 270 nm, indicating the π–π* transition of the aromatic ring (Fig. S4) [34,44]. The XPS spectra (Fig. S5) further confirmed the structure of CD-PNB, with the survey scan featuring peaks centered at 189.0, 284.5, 399.1, 532.1, and 1070.9 eV, which correspond to B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Na1s, respectively. Furthermore, the deconvoluted spectra clearly show the peaks corresponding to C-B, B-O, and quaternized amine (N +) at 283.8, 533.3, and 401.0, respectively, thereby confirming the presence of boronic acid in CD-PNB. The change in the particle size under different pH conditions was analyzed via DLS, and the results are shown in Fig. S6. At pH 7.4, the average diameter of CD-PNB was 53.49 nm, whereas at pH 6.8, the diameter decreased to 15.71 nm, which is almost equal to the size of CD (14.42 nm). This decrease in the particle size was due to the cleavage of the diol–diol bond between the catechol group in the CD and boronic acid in PNB, which was triggered by acidic conditions (pH < 7.4). Furthermore, the surface charge of the nanoparticles was different in the CD and CD-PNB. The zeta potential of CD-PNB was found to be −0.48 mV, which is more positive than that of CD (−20.93 mV) owing to the presence of quaternary amines in PNB and immobilized Na+ (Fig. S7). To understand the optical properties of CD-PNB, PL spectroscopy was performed at different pH and temperatures. As shown in Fig. S8, the PL intensity of CD-PNB at pH 6.8 was higher than that at pH 7.4, owing to the cleavage of the diol–diol bond, which reduced the aggregation caused-quenching (ACQ) effect in CD. Moreover, the PL intensity decreased when the temperature was increased from 25 to 37 °C, owing to the aggregation of temperature-sensitive PNIPAAm in PNB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conductivity and mechanical properties of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The conductivity of the as-prepared CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was examined to understand the changes in its electronic properties at pH 7.4 and 6.8. From the EIS curves and LCR meter, it is evident that the resistance of CD-PNB@PVA at pH 6.8 (4.36 kΩ) was significantly lower than that at pH 7.4 (6.38 kΩ) ( Fig. 1
b,c). The increase of conductivity can be attributed to the breakage of CD and PNB, generating smaller size particle which facilitates the electron transfer process due to shortening of percolation network in CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. The generated smaller nanoparticles possess more surface charge of CD which facilitates the migration of charged species that is responsible for an increase in conductivity [45,46]. Furthermore, this breakage is confirmed by the fluorescence recovery of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel at pH 6.8, which is attributed to the decrease in the ACQ effect of PNB after its cleavage from CD (Fig. S9). Moreover, temperature elevation changed the conductivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. As the temperature was increased from 25 to 60 °C, the resistance of the hydrogel decreased at both pH 7.4 and 6.8 ( Fig. 1
d,e). This change in conductivity depending on the temperature is related to the thermosensitivity of PNIPAAm, which further allow the increase of ionic mobility in hydrogel matrix at higher temperatures. When temperature increased, the PNIPAAm in PNB was aggregated and shrunk due to LCST behavior, causing more packed structure and resulting closer distance between CD-PNB nanoparticles. Thus, this phenomenon will facilitate easier electron transport and ionic mobility, which can increase the conductivity of the hydrogel [13]. The rheological properties of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel were assessed by performing frequency, temperature, and strain sweep tests. From the results of the frequency sweep performed at 25 °C, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel demonstrated the same gel behavior at pH 7.4 and 6.8 (Fig. S10). When a temperature sweep was performed, a viscoelastic transition was observed via a decrease in the storage modulus (G') with an increase in the temperature ( Fig. 1
f). This temperature effect was further examined using the reversible strain sweep mode at 25 and 50 °C ( Figs. 1
g and S11). The G'/G'' ratio clearly indicates that the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was more fluid at a higher temperature (50 °C) than at 25 °C. This can also be inferred from its improved stretchability at higher temperatures (Fig. S12). The photograph showed that the stretchability of hydrogel was improved from 25 °C up to 50 °C, however, the hydrogel started losing the integrity when temperature reached 60 °C. This phenomenon was occurred due to the presence of thermoresponsive PNIPAAm in PNB which affected the aggregation at higher temperature in CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. This change in viscoelasticity at higher temperatures could facilitate an increase in ionic mobility, thereby resulting in the higher conductivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel than at lower temperatures. The other physical properties of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel were examined with respect to the pH conditions. The UTM result revealed that there was no difference in the tensile strength of hydrogels at pH 7.4 and 6.8, achieving stretchability up to approximately 1500% (Fig. S13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strain and pressure sensitivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel produced distinct pressure responses at different pH and temperatures. The pressure response experiments were conducted by performing finger and weight pressing on the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (Figs. S14a and S15). For finger pressing, the pressure response of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel at pH 6.8 was remarkably higher than that at pH 7.4 for all types of applied forces (soft, medium, and hard press) (Fig. S14b,c). These responses were even higher when the temperature was increased from 25 to 37 °C at both pH. Moreover, in the weight pressure experiment (10–250 g), the hydrogel exhibited a pressure response that gradually increased with increasing weight. Furthermore, it demonstrated a higher response by approximately 1.5 times at pH 6.8 than at pH 7.4, at 25 °C ( Fig. 2
a). As the temperature was changed to 37 °C, higher pressure responses were observed at both pH values, which confirms the effect of pH and temperature on pressure sensing ( Fig. 2
b). The distinct strain and pressure sensitivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel at different pH was examined by applying controlled strain and pressure using a UTM and observing the resistance change (ΔR/R0) before and after applying force. Fig. 2
c shows the simultaneous increase in the strain response of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel upon increasing applied strain (0–200% strain) at both pH 7.4 and 6.8. Interestingly, the strain response of the hydrogel at pH 6.8 was higher than that at pH 7.4. This difference in the responses was confirmed by calculating the strain sensitivity (gage factor); the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel exhibited a higher gage factor at pH 6.8 (0.6722) than at pH 7.4 (0.5009), indicating that the hydrogel was more sensitive towards applied strain at pH 6.8 than at pH 7.4 (Fig. S16). Moreover, there was an increase in the pressure response when the applied pressure was increased (0–10 kPa), and a significant difference is observed in the responses of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel at pH 7.4 and 6.8 ( Fig. 2
d). The gage factor for pressure sensitivity was found to be 5.2684 and 4.3064 kPa−1 for the hydrogel at pH 6.8 and 7.4, respectively, confirming the higher sensitivity of the hydrogel towards pressure at pH 6.8 than at pH 7.4 (Fig. S17). Such different responses at each pH are related to the different charge transfer phenomena and conductivities at different pH owing to the cleavage of the diol–diol bond between the CD and PNB.

Further experiments were conducted by applying a simple strain and pressure cycle to the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel at different pH and temperatures. As shown in Fig. 2
e, the hydrogel demonstrated higher responses during the reversible strain cycle when the pH was more acidic (pH 5.0>6.8>7.4), and it increased when the temperature was increased from 25 to 37 °C. Similar to the strain cycle experiment, the weight pressure response was more sensitive when the pH was more acidic (pH 5.0>6.8>7.4) and temperature was higher ( Fig. 2
f). This was due to the different mobility of charge transfer resulting from the breakage of CD-PNB and the increasing viscoelasticity of the hydrogel matrix at higher temperatures. For ease of detection, the strain and pressure response of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel can be monitored via a wireless sensing system, allowing real-time data to be received on a smartphone (Fig. S18) [47]. As can be seen in Fig. 2
g, distinct strain responses were obtained for the hydrogel at different pH (5.0, 6.8, and 7.4) and temperatures. The results were similar and a clear gap was present between each parameter, as observed from the sourcemeter measurement ( Fig. 2
e). Furthermore, this wireless monitoring approach could provide real-time pressure responses ( Fig. 2
h), and the values obtained were comparable with those obtained using the previous sourcemeter method ( Fig. 2
f).

The conductivity of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel were consisted of electrical conductivity and ionic conductivity from the presence of Na+. To prove the electrical and ionic conductivity in the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel, LCR meter (AC system) and sourcemeter (DC system) were further used to check CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel in pH 6.8 and 7.4, and the results between those instruments were compared. As shown in Fig. S19, the resistance values of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel were found to be 4.36 kΩ (pH 6.8) and 6.38 kΩ (pH 7.4) in LCR meter, while from the sourcemeter, the resistance values were 5.48 kΩ (pH 6.8) and 7.64 kΩ (pH 7.4). As the LCR meter was used to evaluate the AC measurement condition, while the sourcemeter was used for DC measurement, the deviation of results was occurred. In LCR meter, the measured conductivity came from electrical and ionic conductivity. On the other hand, sourcemeter can only measure the electrical conductivity. Furthermore, LCR meter and sourcemeter were used to check pressure response of CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel, and the result was also compared. As shown in Fig. S20, there was no significant different in pressure response results between LCR meter and sourcemeter. This was because for measurement of pressure or strain response, the result was calculated as a ratio between initial resistance and final resistance (ΔR/R0) when the force was applied on CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. Thus, the use of LCR meter or sourcemeter in this strain and pressure sensing measurement can be comparable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer cell-selective strain and pressure response of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: As the microenvironment of cancer cells is more acidic (pH < 6.8) than that of normal cells (pH 7.4), the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel is expected to generate different strain and pressure responses in cancer cells and normal cells. For the cell experiment, a live and dead assay was initially conducted to ensure the biocompatibility of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel by seeding MDCK (normal cells) and HeLa cells (cancer cells) into the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. Flow cytometry and confocal imaging revealed that almost 100% of the cells were alive even after incubation for 3 days (Fig. S21a,b), indicating that the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel demonstrated excellent biocompatibility towards MDCK and HeLa cells. Hence, this CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel can be used for further in vitro experiments. Moreover, to ensure that the cells were well-seeded into the hydrogel, SEM imaging was performed. The SEM image clearly shows the presence of cells in the pores of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel, indicating successful cell seeding into the hydrogel (Fig. S22a). The SEM image captured after removing the cells from the hydrogel shows that no cells remained attached to the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (Fig. S22b). EIS measurements further show that the untreated hydrogel and cell-treated hydrogel before and after washing process exhibited different conductivities. Compared to the untreated hydrogel, cell-treated hydrogel before washing process showed higher resistance as the cells were embedded in the hydrogel. The higher resistance found in cell-treated hydrogel before wash can be attributed to the additional resistance from the presence of cells which contains non-conductive complex cellular compounds. When the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was then washed, the resistance was significantly lower than that before washing as the cells were successfully removed from the hydrogel (Fig. S23).

The variation in the conductivity of the cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was determined with respect to the incubation time (0–3 d) after removing the cells from the hydrogel ( Figs. 3
a–d and S24). HeLa and PC-3 cells were used as representative cancer cells, and MDCK and CHO-K1 cells were used as the control (normal cells). The resistance of HeLa- and PC-3-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogels decreased with an increase in incubation time because of the decrease in pH as a result of the increase in the number of cancer cells after 3 d of incubation (Table S1). In contrast, no remarkable change in resistance was observed in the MDCK- and CHO-K1-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel after 3 d of incubation, as there was no change in the pH. Based on these results, we tested the strain and pressure sensitivity of the cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel using HeLa and MDCK cells after incubation for 1 d As shown in Fig. 3
e, the strain response (0–200% strain) of the HeLa-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was remarkably higher than that of the MDCK-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel, and the strain sensitivities were 0.7439 and 0.5009, respectively (Fig. S25). A higher pressure response was obtained for the HeLa-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel than for the MDCK-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel in the pressure experiment ( Fig. 3
f), which is supported by the higher gage factor of the HeLa-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (5.3052 kPa−1) than that of the MDCK-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (4.2720 kPa−1) (Fig. S26). Furthermore, we assessed the change in strain and pressure response with respect to the incubation time (0–3 d) using HeLa and PC-3 cells for cancer cell lines, and MDCK and CHO-K1 for normal cell lines ( Fig. 3
g,h). With a longer incubation time, the strain and pressure response of the cancer cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel increased, whereas those of the normal cell-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel remained the same as the initial values. These phenomena were further monitored via a wireless system, and we found that the strain–pressure responses after treatment with HeLa and PC-3 cells were significantly higher than those after treatment with MDCK and CHO-K1 cells ( Figs. 3
i,j and S27).

All these results confirm the selectivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel towards cancer cells compared to normal cells, owing to the different pH in the microenvironment of each cell. To verify that the extracellular pH of the cell is the main driving force for a distinct strain–pressure responses, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was treated with cancer cells and normal cells in media with different pH values (7.4, 6.8, and 6.0) (Fig. S28a). The EIS results showed that the resistance of both HeLa- and MDCK-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogels decreased as the pH of the media decreased (Fig. S28b,c). However, the resistance of the HeLa-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was still lower than that of the MDCK-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. Moreover, the strain and pressure response of the hydrogel toward both the types of cells increased when the media pH was lower, indicating that the pH condition was the main factor responsible for the distinct conductivity and strain–pressure response in the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (Fig. S29a,b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study of tumor microenvironment-selective wireless touch sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The selectivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel towards tumor microenvironment was examined by implanting the hydrogel into the tumor site in tumor-bearing mice. Three days after the implantation, two approaches were utilized to measure the distinct conductivity in tumor-bearing mice and normal mice, including in situ and ex situ monitoring of resistance using a wireless sensing system. For in situ wireless monitoring, a wireless device was prepared and connected directly to the implanted site, as shown in Fig. 4
a. In situ wireless monitoring showed that the resistance of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel implanted in tumor-bearing mice was lower than that of the hydrogel before implantation and the hydrogel in normal mice. Ex situ wireless monitoring was conducted by harvesting the implanted hydrogel and subsequently connecting it to the wireless device ( Fig. 4
b). The results obtained via ex situ wireless monitoring were similar to those obtained via the in situ method, indicating a significant change in resistance in the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel implanted in tumor-bearing mice. The conductivities of the implanted CD-PNB@PVA hydrogels were further checked ex situ via EIS and a sourcemeter. The EIS data revealed a change in conductivity after implantation for 3 d Compared with the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel before implantation (control), the hydrogel implanted in tumor-bearing mice showed a significant decrease in resistance, whereas only a slight change was observed in the resistance of the hydrogel implanted in normal mice ( Fig. 4
c). Moreover, sourcemeter results showed that as the implantation time increased, the resistance of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel in tumor-bearing mice decreased significantly compared to that of the hydrogel implanted in normal mice (control) ( Fig. 4
d). Furthermore, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel implanted in tumor-bearing mice demonstrated a more sensitive response to pressure than the hydrogel before implantation (control) and the hydrogel implanted in normal mice ( Fig. 4
e). A wireless sensing system was also used to monitor the pressure sensing after harvesting the implanted hydrogel. The results clearly indicate that the pressure sensitivity of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel in tumor-bearing mice was higher than that of the control and the hydrogel implanted in normal mice ( Fig. 4
f). Therefore, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel is proven to be sensitive to tumor microenvironment and demonstrated excellent performance in vivo in distinguishing tumor from normal tissue. To confirm that the implantation of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel did not cause inflammation in mice, a histological analysis was performed after 2 weeks of implantation. As shown in Fig. 4
g, H&E staining showed no significant immune response in mouse tissue, indicating that no inflammation occurred during the implantation of the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. Thus, this tumor microenvironment-selective touch sensor is not only biocompatible but also effective and sensitive for strain- and pressure-based tumor detection.

We successfully developed a tumor-selective touch sensor based on pH-responsive nanoparticles-embedded hydrogel, which produced distinct conductivity and electronic strain–pressure responses to the conditions in a tumor microenvironment. The acidic condition of tumor microenvironment is a driving force for producing the tumor-selective strain–pressure response, owing to the cleavage of diol–diol crosslinking between the catechol in semiconducting CD and the boronic acid group in non-conductive temperature-sensitive PNB. The CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel demonstrated higher conductivity and strain–pressure responses at pH 6.8 than at pH 7.4, which became more significant when the temperature was increased. This increase was because of the different charge transfer and ionic mobility in acidic pH after the separation of semiconducting CD nanoparticles and non-conductive temperature-sensitive PNB, which decreased the hindrance to the charge transfer process in the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel. The CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards HeLa and PC-3 cancer cells, which is indicated by its 2 times higher ΔR/R0 value under applied strain and pressure (125% and 56% for HeLa; 133% and 63% for PC-3, respectively) compared with that of MDCK- (70% and 33%, respectively) and CHO-K1-treated CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel (70% and 34%, respectively). Moreover, in vivo experiments in tumor-bearing mice revealed that the implanted CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel demonstrated excellent performance in detecting tumor in situ and ex situ, with a more sensitive response towards tumor than towards normal tissue. To simplify the monitoring process, the CD-PNB@PVA hydrogel was connected to a wireless sensing system that produced real-time conductivity and strain–pressure profiles on a smartphone. Thus, this tumor-selective strain–pressure hydrogel-based sensor offers a facile approach for sensitive and selective tumor detection and possesses potential for application in point-of-care diagnostics.
